mnemonics: VAR Is A Lazy Namer (V.I.A.L.N.): 'var' Infers A Local Name, reminding you it only works for Local Variable declarations with Initializers.
keyPoints: The 'var' keyword allows the compiler to infer the type of a local variable based on its initializer.
You cannot use 'var' for method parameters, return types, or instance/class variables.
Inheritance allows a subclass to inherit fields and methods from a superclass, enabling code reuse and polymorphism.
A variable of a superclass type can hold a reference to an object of any subclass (Polymorphism).
When using 'var' with inheritance, the inferred type is the specific class of the object, not the abstract superclass type.
commonMistakes: Trying to use 'var' without an initializer (e.g., 'var x;'). The compiler has nothing to infer from.
Reassigning a 'var' variable with a different, incompatible type. The type is fixed after declaration.
Using 'var' for a class field, which is not allowed. It's for local variables only.
Confusing the reference type with the object type in inheritance. The 'var' keyword will infer the precise object type, not the general reference type you might expect.
